26: ein kaEl Yeshurun rokev shamayim be'ezrecha uvegeavo shechaqim (ʾên kāʾēl yĕšurûn rōkēb šāmayim bĕʿezrekkā ûbĕgaʾăwātô šĕḥāqîm): 'There is none like God, O Jeshurun, who rides through the heavens to your help, through the skies in his majesty.' The incomparability formula (ein kaEl, there is none like God) applied to YHWH as the celestial rider — rokev shamayim, the one who rides the heavens. Psalm 68:33-34 develops the same image: Sing to God who rides in the ancient heavens; praise his strength. Ephesians 4:8 cites Psalm 68:18 (the rider who ascends and distributes gifts) in a Christological reading. The celestial rider of Deut 33:26 and Psalm 68 becomes the ascending and gift-giving Christ of Ephesians 4, and finally the rider on the white horse of Revelation 19:11-16 (Faithful and True, whose name is the Word of God).
27: me'onah Elohei qedem umitachat zero'ot olam vayegaresh mipaneicha oyev vayomer hashmeid (mĕʿōnāh ʾĕlōhê qedem ûmittaḥat zĕrōʿōt ʿôlām waygāreš mippānêkā ʾôyēb wayyōʾmer hašmēd): 'The eternal God is your dwelling place, and underneath are the everlasting arms. And he thrust out the enemy before you and said, Destroy.' Elohei qedem (God of old/ancient God) — YHWH as the primal, eternal divine being who preceded all creation. Zerovot olam (everlasting/eternal arms): the arms of YHWH as the foundation beneath Israel, both the shelter that surrounds and the support that sustains. The image is of a parent whose arms are always under the child who might fall. The NT equivalent is John 10:28-29 (no one can snatch them out of my hand... no one can snatch them out of the Father's hand) — the same absolute security mediated by divine power rather than creature strength.
1: wĕzōʾt habbĕrākhāh ʾăšer bērakh mōšeh ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm — 'This is the blessing with which Moses the man of God blessed the sons of Israel.' The superscription frames the passage as the final act of Moses's prophetic-priestly role: ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm (man of God — H376, H430 — a prophetic title for one who has direct access to YHWH, used of Elijah, Elisha, Samuel) is the highest honorific in Israel's prophetic tradition. The blessing (bĕrākhāh — H1293) was the patriarchal prerogative Moses carried as Israel's leader. Like Jacob's blessing of the twelve sons (Gen 49), Moses's blessing reviewed each tribe's character and destiny — but from the perspective of YHWH's election rather than merely genealogical traits.
2: YHWH missînay bāʾ wĕzāraḥ miśśēʿîr lāmô — 'YHWH came from Sinai and dawned from Seir upon them.' The theophanic hymn that opens Moses's blessing is one of the oldest Hebrew poems: YHWH as the divine warrior marching to Israel's aid from Sinai (sînay), Seir (śēʿîr), and Mount Paran (har pārān). The three directional references correspond to the Sinai peninsula region. The word zāraḥ (H2224 — to rise, shine like the sun) applies the solar imagery of divine theophany. ʾātāh mirĕḇĕḇōt qōdeš (he came from the holy myriads — from Sinai's ten-thousands of holy ones) identifies the angelic/covenant retinue accompanying YHWH. The parallel in Judg 5:4-5 (Deborah's Song) and Hab 3:3-4 shows this was a standard Israelite theophanic tradition.
3: ʾaf ḥōḇēḇ ʿammîm kol-qĕdōšāyw bĕyādekā — 'Indeed, he loves his peoples; all his holy ones are in your hand.' Ḥōḇēḇ (H2245 — to cherish, love warmly) — a rare verb found only here — describes YHWH's affectionate love for Israel. The phrase kol-qĕdōšāyw bĕyādekā (all his holy ones are in your hand) places all who belong to YHWH under His sovereign protection. The subsequent phrase hēm tukku lĕraglikhā yiśśā middabbĕrōtêkhā — 'they bowed down at your feet, accepted your words' — describes Israel's covenant submission at Sinai: the people who received Torah bowed before YHWH as covenant suzerain.
4: tôrāh tsiwwāh-lānû mōšeh mōrāšāh qĕhillat yaʿăqōḇ — 'Moses commanded us the Torah, a possession/heritage of the congregation of Jacob.' The verse is a liturgical formula recited in Israel — mōrāšāh (H4181 — possession, inheritance, heritage; from H3423 yāraš, to inherit/possess) frames Torah not as an external imposition but as Israel's family inheritance. Qĕhillat yaʿăqōḇ (the congregation/assembly of Jacob — H6951) identifies the community as the covenantal gathering of Jacob's descendants. The Torah was the family document of YHWH's household — as much a possession as the land itself.
5: vayhî ḇîšurûn melek — 'He became king in Jeshurun.' The kingship of YHWH over Israel (melek — H4428) was established at the covenant assembly at Sinai: bĕhitʾassep rāʾšê ʿām yaḥad šiḇṭê yisrāʾel (when the heads of the people assembled, the tribes of Israel together). The Sinai theophany was Israel's coronation of YHWH as king — the covenant ratification was a formal acknowledgment of YHWH's sovereignty. This verse anticipates the later debates about human kingship (1 Sam 8:7: 'they have not rejected you, but they have rejected me from being king over them') — YHWH was always king; human kings were his vice-regents.
6: yĕhî rĕʾûḇēn weʾal-yāmōt ûyĕhî mĕṯāyw mispar — 'Let Reuben live and not die, though his men be few.' The brevity of Reuben's blessing reflects the tribe's historical trajectory: forfeiting the firstborn's double portion after the sin with Bilhah (Gen 35:22; 49:4), and the tribe's later instability and modest size. Yĕhî rĕʾûḇēn (let Reuben live) is a petition, not a declaration — Moses prayed for a tribe that was in danger of disappearing. The phrase ûyĕhî mĕṯāyw mispar (his men few in number) is either a description of their current state or a qualification of the blessing: even with few men, let them live.
7: wĕzōʾt lîhûdāh vayyōʾmar — 'And this for Judah; he said.' Judah's blessing is a military prayer: šĕmaʿ YHWH qôl yĕhûdāh (hear, YHWH, the voice of Judah) and weʾel-ʿammô tĕḇîʾennû (bring him to his people). The hands of Judah contend (yādāyw rāḇ-lô) and YHWH is asked to be his helper against enemies (weʿēzer missārāyw tihyeh). The blessing anticipates Judah's military leadership in Israel's history (Judg 1:1-2 — Judah goes first) and the Davidic dynasty that would emerge from the tribe. The prayer for Judah to return to his people may reflect a historical circumstance of isolation in battle.
8: ûlĕlēwî ʾāmar tummêkhā weʾûrîkhā lĕʾîš ḥăsîdêkhā — 'Of Levi he said: Your Thummim and Urim belong to your godly one.' The Levitical blessing centers on the priestly instruments: tummêkhā weʾûrîkhā (your Thummim and Urim — H8550, H224, the lots for divine oracle in the high priest's breastplate). Ḥăsîdêkhā (your godly/faithful one — H2623 from H2617 ḥesed) refers to Levi as YHWH's covenant-loyal servant. The historical basis: Massah (nissîtô bemassāh) and Meribah (tĕrîḇēhû ʿal-mê mĕrîḇāh) — where Levi (cf. Exod 32:26-29 — the golden calf incident) proved covenant loyalty by standing with YHWH against idolaters, even family members.
9: hāʾōmēr lĕʾāḇîw ûlĕʾimmô lōʾ rĕʾîtîw — 'Who said of his father and mother, “I do not see them.”' The Levitical commitment to YHWH surpassed family ties: lōʾ rĕʾîtîw (I do not see him), weʾet-ʾeḥāyw lōʾ hikhîr (did not acknowledge his brothers), weʾet-bānāyw lōʾ yādāʿ (did not know his children). This absolute loyalty — the same radical commitment Jesus demanded for discipleship (Matt 10:37; Luke 14:26) — was demonstrated at the golden calf incident when the Levites 'consecrated themselves to YHWH that day at the cost of sons and brothers' (Exod 32:29). The Levitical priestly vocation required the displacement of all other loyalties by covenant loyalty to YHWH.
10: yôrû mishpāṭêkhā lĕyaʿăqōḇ wetôrāthkhā lĕyisrāʾel — 'They will teach your ordinances to Jacob and your Torah to Israel.' The Levitical three-fold ministry: teaching (yōrû — H3384, to teach, direct; the root of tôrāh), offering incense (yāśîmû qĕṭôrāh bĕʾappêkhā), and burnt offerings (wekhālîl ʿal-mizbeḥekhā). The teaching ministry of the Levites was the mechanism of covenant transmission — they were the living library of Israel's law (2 Chr 17:7-9; Neh 8:7-9; Mal 2:6-7). The prophetic complaint against priests who failed to teach the Torah (Hos 4:6; Mal 2:8) shows how crucial this function was to Israel's covenant continuity.
11: bārēkh YHWH ḥêlô ûfoʿal yādāyw tirtsāh — 'Bless YHWH his wealth and accept the work of his hands.' The prayer for Levitical blessing encompasses both resources (ḥayil — wealth, capacity, substance) and labor (poʿal yādāyw — the work of his hands, the priestly service). The imprecation against enemies: mehātsats mĕqāmāyw ûmeśōnĕʾāyw min-yĕqûmûn (shatter the loins of his attackers and those who hate him, so they do not rise) echoes the protection of the priestly office itself. Those who opposed Levi's ministry — as Korah (Num 16), Saul (1 Sam 13), Uzziah (2 Chr 26) — would find their opposition divinely crushed.
12: lĕḇinyāmîn ʾāmar yĕdîd YHWH yiškōn lāḇeṭaḥ ʿālāyw — 'Of Benjamin he said: The beloved of YHWH dwells in safety beside him.' Yĕdîd YHWH (beloved of YHWH — H3039 from H1730, love) gives Benjamin YHWH's personal affection — the tribe that would carry the temple in Jerusalem on its territory. Yiškōn lāḇeṭaḥ ʿālāyw (he dwells in security beside him) and ḥōpēp ʿālāyw kol-hayyôm (he covers/shields him all the day long) and ûḇên kĕtēpāyw šākhēn (he dwells between his shoulders/flanks) — the last phrase locates YHWH's dwelling between Benjamin's mountain ridges, anticipating the Jerusalem temple which sat on the Benjamin-Judah border.
13: ûlĕyôsēp ʾāmar mĕḇōreket YHWH ʾartsô — 'Of Joseph he said: Blessed by YHWH is his land.' Joseph's blessing is the most lavishly detailed, covering geography (heavens, earth, depths), time (perennial fruit, every month), and cosmic abundance. Mimmeghed šāmayim miṭṭāl (from the bounty of heaven's dew), ûmittĕhôm rōḇetset tāḥat (from the deep lying beneath) — the blessing comes from above (rain/dew) and below (springs/groundwater). The fertile Manasseh and Ephraim territories in the central highlands (modern West Bank and Jezreel Valley) were indeed among the most productive agricultural regions in Canaan.
14: ûmimmeghed tĕḇûʾōt šāmeš — 'From the bounty of the produce of the sun.' The solar blessing (tĕḇûʾōt šāmeš — sun-ripened harvest) and lunar blessing (meghed garēš yĕrāḥîm — bounty yielded by the moon) refer to the agricultural rhythms governed by celestial bodies. The complete round of the agricultural year — spring rains from the heavens, summer sun ripening, autumn harvest, winter depths supplying groundwater — was YHWH's gift to Joseph's land. The verse reflects sophisticated agricultural understanding: both solar radiation for ripening and lunar cycles governing tidal/rainfall patterns contributed to the land's productivity.
15: ûmerōʾš harĕrê-qedem ûmimmeghed giḇĕʿôt ʿôlām — 'From the finest of the ancient mountains, the bounty of the age-old hills.' The mountains (harĕrê-qedem — ancient mountains, from the beginning of time) and everlasting hills (giḇĕʿôt ʿôlām) provided the mineral richness and forest resources that supplemented the valley agriculture. The phrase echoes Gen 49:26 (Jacob's blessing of Joseph: 'bounties of the ancient mountains, the age-old hills'). The geologically ancient landscape of Canaan — limestone ridges, fertile valleys, springs — was Joseph's inheritance, and its abundance was traced to YHWH's creation of the land from primordial times.
16: ûmimmeghed ʾerets ûmĕlôʾāh ûrĕtsôn šōkhĕnî sĕneh — 'From the bounty of the earth and its fullness, and from the favor of the one who dwelt in the bush.' The reference to šōkhĕnî sĕneh (the dweller in the bush — H5572, the burning bush of Exod 3) is extraordinary: YHWH appeared to Moses in the thorn-bush of the desert, and this theophany on behalf of Joseph's tribes is now invoked for their blessing. The connection: YHWH met Moses in the wilderness to announce Israel's redemption from Egypt — that same divine favor (rĕtsôn — H7522, favor, goodwill, acceptable pleasure) would rest on Joseph's territory. The theophany-blessing connection honored both the means of Exodus revelation and its beneficiaries.
17: bekôr šôrô hādār lô weqarnê rĕʾēm qarnāyw — 'A firstborn bull — majesty is his! His horns are the horns of a wild ox.' The bull-imagery for Ephraim (šôr — H7794, bull/ox, symbol of power and agricultural strength) was YHWH's chosen imagery for Joseph's supremacy. Rĕʾēm (H7214 — wild ox, the aurochs, the most formidable bovine) with its qarnāyw (horns — H7161) would gore (yenaggaḥ) peoples together. The military application: ʾappê-ʾereṣ yaḥdāw (pushing the peoples to the ends of the earth). The ten thousands of Ephraim (rĕḇāḇôt ʾeprayim) and the thousands of Manasseh (ʾalpê manašše) were the numerical legacy of Joseph's blessing — the largest tribal census counts in Num 1 and 26.
18: ûlizĕḇûlun ʾāmar śĕmaḥ zĕḇûlun bĕṣēʾtekhā — 'Of Zebulun he said: Rejoice, Zebulun, in your going out.' The twin blessing of Zebulun and Issachar (Gen 49:13-14) reflects their geographic and economic partnership: Zebulun's bĕṣēʾtekhā (going out — maritime/trade ventures) and Issachar's bĕʾohālêkhā (in your tents — agricultural stability). The pairing celebrates the complementarity of trade and farming: Zebulun's sea commerce (yirʾû — they will suck the wealth of the sea) and Issachar's land productivity. The reference to safûn yammîm (the hidden treasures of the sand) likely refers to glassmaking sand from the Carmel coast, an important trade commodity in antiquity.
19: ʿammîm har-yiqrāʾû šāmmāh yizbeḥû ziḇḥê-tsedeq — 'They will summon peoples to the mountain; there they will offer right sacrifices.' The mountain (har) where peoples are summoned to worship anticipates Zion theology (Isa 2:3; Mic 4:2 — 'let us go up to the mountain of YHWH'). Ziḇḥê-tsedeq (sacrifices of righteousness — H6664) are the properly offered, covenantally correct sacrifices (cf. Ps 4:5; 51:19). The Zebulun-Issachar region sucked the wealth of the seas (šĕfaq yammîm yinyaqû) and the hidden treasures of the sand (ûśĕfûnê tĕmûnê ḥôl) — indicating a prosperous region that would support proper worship.
20: ûlĕgād ʾāmar bārûkh marḥîḇ gād — 'Of Gad he said: Blessed is he who enlarges Gad.' The blessing for Gad emphasizes territorial expansion (marḥîḇ — H7337, to widen, enlarge) and military prowess: Gad dwells like a lion (kĕlāḇîʾ šākhēn), tearing arm and crown of the head (veyiṭrap zĕrôaʿ ʾap-qodqōd). The verb ṭārap (H2963 — to tear, rend — the lion's attack) portrays Gad as a fierce warrior tribe. The first portion (rēʾšît lô) was taken for Gad — possibly referring to their early conquest east of the Jordan (Num 32) or the firstfruits of the spoil. Gad's territory in Gilead was frontier land requiring both military strength and territorial aggression.
21: vayyarʾ rēʾšît lô kî šām ḥelqat mĕḥōqēq sāfûn — 'He chose the best land for himself, for a commander's portion was reserved for him.' Mĕḥōqēq (H2710 — lawgiver, commander, the one who enacts decrees) in Gad's territory may allude to Moses's burial there (Deut 34:6 — on the east side of the Jordan) or to a leader born from Gad. The verse ends with praise: wayyēṯē rāʾšê ʿām tsidqat YHWH weʾet-mišpāṭāyw ʿim-yisrāʾel (he came with the heads of the people, executed YHWH's righteousness and his ordinances with Israel). Gad's loyalty to the trans-Jordan covenant commitment (Josh 22) validated this blessing.
22: ûlĕdān ʾāmar dān gûr ʾaryēh yĕzanneq min-habbāšān — 'Of Dan he said: Dan is a lion's cub that leaps from Bashan.' The Dan blessing uses the lion-cub imagery (gûr ʾaryēh — H1482 gûr, young animal; H738 ʾaryēh, lion) that Gen 49:9 applied to Judah. Dan's migration from their original southern allotment to Bashan in the far north (Judg 17-18) was apparently anticipated here — the tribe that leapt northward to find new territory. Yĕzanneq (H2187 — to leap, spring — a hapax legomenon) suggests sudden movement. Dan's military agility and territorial flexibility made them a distinct tribe, though their ultimate apostasy (setting up idols at Laish/Dan) was the shadow over their blessing.
23: ûlĕnaptālî ʾāmar naptālî śĕbaʿ rātsôn ûmālēʾ birkhat YHWH — 'Of Naphtali he said: Naphtali, satisfied with favor, full of the blessing of YHWH.' The three-fold abundance: śāḇaʿ rātsôn (satisfied with favor — H7646, H7522), mālēʾ birkhat YHWH (full of YHWH's blessing — H4390), and possession of yam wedarôm yrāšāh (the sea and the south take possession). The 'sea' likely refers to the Sea of Galilee (Kinneret) on Naphtali's western border, and the 'south' to its territory extending southward. Naphtali's territory included some of the most fertile land in the Galilee region — the Kinnereth basin, source of abundant fish and agricultural produce.
24: ûlĕʾāšēr ʾāmar bārûkh mibbānîm ʾāšēr — 'Of Asher he said: Most blessed of sons is Asher.' Asher's blessing is triple: most blessed among sons (bārûkh mibbānîm), favored by brothers (yĕhî rĕtsûy ʾeḥāyw), and destined to dip his foot in oil (weṭōḇēl bašemen raglô). The olive oil (šemen) for which the Asher coastal territory was famous (the modern Carmel coast and western Galilee) is legendary — still today the finest olives in the region grow along this coast. The combination of material prosperity (barezel ûnĕḥōšet — iron and bronze sandals for strength, v25) and covenant security made Asher's tribal portion one of the most desirable.
25: barzel ûnĕḥōšet mĕnaʿălêkhā ûkeḏāḇrekhā yaḏmôtekhā — 'Your bars shall be iron and bronze; as your days, so your strength.' The promise to Asher has two parts: barzel ûnĕḥōšet mĕnaʿălêkhā (iron and bronze are your shoes/bolts — either sandals or door-bolts, symbolizing impregnable defense) and keḏāḇrekhā yaḏmôtekhā (as your days, so your strength — literally 'as your day, so your rest/security'). The formula keḏāḇrekhā (as your day) means: whatever each day demands, YHWH provides the corresponding strength. This promise was later universalized by Paul (Phil 4:19: 'my God will supply every need of yours according to his riches in glory') and is the model for covenant sufficiency.
28: wayyiškōn yisrāʾēl beṭaḥ bādād ʿên yaʿăqōḇ — 'Israel lived in safety, Jacob in security alone.' The closing vision of Israel's blessed state: beṭaḥ bādād (in security, alone — H983, H910) — the security of those who need no international alliances, dwelling safely because YHWH alone is their protector. The phrase ʾên yaʿăqōḇ (the spring/eye of Jacob) is ambiguous but likely refers to YHWH as the living spring sustaining Jacob. The agricultural bounty: ʾel ʾereṣ dāgān wĕṯîrôš (to a land of grain and new wine), ʾap-šāmāyw yaʿărpû ṭāl (whose heavens drip with dew). The land is the material expression of YHWH's covenant faithfulness to the patriarchal promises.
29: ʾašrêkhā yisrāʾēl mî khāmôkhā — 'Blessed are you, O Israel! Who is like you?' The macarism (ʾašrêkhā — H835, blessed, happy — the plural of fullness, comprehensive wellbeing) closes the entire blessing collection. The rhetorical question mî khāmôkhā (who is like you?) echoes Exod 15:11 (mî khāmōkhā bāʾēlim YHWH — who is like you among the gods, O YHWH) — Israel's uniqueness mirrors YHWH's uniqueness: both are incomparable in their respective spheres. The reason: ʿam nôšaʿ baYHWH (a people saved by YHWH) and māgēn ʿezrekā (shield of your help) and ʾăšer-ḥereb gaʾwātekā (the sword of your majesty). Israel's greatness was entirely derivative — it was great because its God was great, saved because its Savior was YHWH.
